summary.what: Tune the remaining six builtin draft templates for artifact-appropriate signal
summary.why: Each artifact has different signal needs — applying devblog-style operator-voice everywhere would be wrong (changelog, release notes), but each template had its own dryness or fabrication risk that the v1-v5 versions didn't address.
summary.how: Per-template diagnosis: changelog needed Added/Changed disambiguation + consolidation + strict exclusion list; decision-log needed Status/Date/supersession + operator-intent in Context; pr-description needed size adaptation + collaboration callout + test-plan honesty; release-notes needed strict 'user-observable' filter + 'what should I do' for breaking changes + anti-fabrication; sprint-report needed Friction/Carry-overs + retro fodder + Highlights criteria; standup needed Asks for help + time-burn texture.

notes: Resisted the temptation to inject operator-voice uniformly. Changelog and release-notes are deliberately neutral artifacts — readers want a list of facts at a specific abstraction level. Pushing operator-voice there would feel performative and miss what those readers actually need. PR description, ADR Context, sprint-report, and standup all benefit from collaboration awareness because their readers (reviewers, future maintainers, PMs, teammates) calibrate differently when they know how the work was actually built. The Status field on ADRs is the most structurally significant change — without it, decision logs accumulate without any way to capture reversals; agents have no scaffolding for noting 'this was superseded by X.' Considered adding a structured 'agent_involvement: high|medium|low' field to entries — rejected: too much taxonomy, encourages fabrication; the existing notes field does this fine when used well. Test-plan honesty in PR description is the change most likely to push back on real LLM laziness — 'Tests pass' as a fake verified claim is an actual failure mode, and naming it explicitly is the cheapest counter.
